title: South Korea nears completion of five-satellite military reconnaissance network
summary: South Korea's fifth military reconnaissance satellite completed operational testing ahead of schedule, finalizing the 425 Project constellation designed to independently monitor North Korean nuclear and missile facilities.
contents:
## South Korea Completes Military Satellite Network

### Milestone Achievement
South Korea's fifth military reconnaissance satellite — launched in November 2025 from Cape Canaveral — completed all operational testing requirements and now enters full service by end of April 2026, approximately two months ahead of schedule.

### 425 Project Overview
The milestone completes the long-running "425 Project," named for its satellite composition: four synthetic aperture radar (SAR) satellites and one electro-optical/infrared (EO/IR) satellite. Seoul first pursued the program in 2013 and approved its implementation strategy in 2017. SpaceX supported all five launches between late 2023 and late 2025.

### Enhanced Capabilities
The five-satellite constellation provides South Korea with high-resolution imagery at roughly two-hour intervals, enabling surveillance regardless of weather or time of day. The system forms a key component of the "Kill Chain" preemptive strike framework — designed to detect and neutralize signs of imminent North Korean weapons of mass destruction use.

### Strategic Independence
The deployment marks a shift away from reliance on US intelligence toward independent surveillance capabilities. South Korea plans to deploy around 20 small satellites and 40 microsatellites by 2030 to reduce revisit times to under 30 minutes, addressing the current two-hour gap that North Korean mobile launchers could exploit.
